label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out this locker problem. So, there are 100 lockers, all starting closed. Then, 100 people do something with them. Person 1 opens every locker. Person 2 toggles every 2nd locker. Person k toggles every k-th locker, and this goes on until k is 100. The question is how many lockers are open at the end. Hmm. Alright, let's start by understanding what "toggle" means. If a locker is open, toggling it would close it, and if it's closed, toggling would open it. So each person is flipping the state of certain lockers. Person 1 opens all lockers. Then Person 2 closes every 2nd locker because they start off open. Then Person 3 toggles every 3rd locker. And this continues up to Person 100. I need to figure out how many times each locker is toggled. Because a locker starts closed, and each toggle changes its state. If a locker is toggled an odd number of times, it will end up open, and if it's toggled even times, it'll be closed. So the problem reduces to finding how many lockers have an odd number of toggles. Now, the number of times a locker is toggled depends on how many people toggle it. For a particular locker number, say locker number n, it's toggled by every person k where k is a divisor of n. Because Person k toggles every k-th locker, so if k divides n (i.e., n is a multiple of k), then locker n is toggled by person k. Therefore, the number of times locker n is toggled is equal to the number of divisors of n. For example, locker 6 is toggled by persons 1, 2, 3, and 6. So divisors of 6 are 1, 2, 3, 6. Therefore, it's toggled 4 times, which is even, so it ends up closed. So the problem now is to find how many numbers from 1 to 100 have an odd number of divisors. Because those lockers will be open. So why do some numbers have an odd number of divisors? Well, divisors usually come in pairs. For example, for 6, the divisors are (1,6) and (2,3). Each pair multiplies to 6. However, if a number is a perfect square, like 16, one of its divisors is repeated. For example, the divisors of 16 are 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. Here, 4 is the square root of 16. In this case, the divisor 4 is only counted once, so the total number of divisors becomes odd. So perfect squares have an odd number of divisors, and all other numbers have an even number of divisors. Therefore, the lockers that
